title: Score Distillation Sampling, Conceptually
concept: Score Distillation Sampling optimizes a 3D representation so that its 2D renders from many angles all look plausible to a 2D diffusion model.

exercises.prompt: You want to build a 3D statue, but you only have an art critic who judges flat photos. How could the critic still help you sculpt a good 3D statue?

options.text: Show the critic photos of the statue from many angles and keep sculpting until every photo looks good
Ask the critic to guess the statue's weight
Only show the critic one photo and stop

exercises.explain: If every 2D photo of your statue looks convincing to the critic, chances are the 3D statue itself is convincing too!

cards.title: Sculpting with a 2D Judge
cards.body: Imagine you don't have a 3D expert, but you DO have an amazing judge of 2D pictures (a diffusion model). Could that judge still help you create an amazing 3D object? Turns out... yes! That's the magic trick behind Score Distillation Sampling.

cards.title: What is Score Distillation Sampling?
cards.body: Score Distillation Sampling (SDS) is a way to build a 3D object by repeatedly rendering it from random camera angles, asking a pretrained 2D diffusion model 'does this look real?', and nudging the 3D shape/colors so that ALL those 2D views look more plausible.

cards.title: The Photo Booth Feedback Loop
cards.body: Picture a clay figure spinning slowly in front of a photo booth. After every snapshot, an expert whispers 'make this angle look a bit more like a real dragon.' You adjust the clay a tiny bit. Do this from hundreds of angles, over and over, and the clay slowly becomes a dragon that looks right from EVERY side — even though the expert only ever looked at flat photos.

cards.title: Text-to-3D Dragon
cards.body: You type 'a red dragon.' The system starts with a random blobby 3D shape. It renders the blob from a random angle, asks a 2D diffusion model trained on dragon images how to make that render look more like a dragon, and uses that hint to adjust the 3D shape a little. Repeat thousands of times from thousands of angles, and the blob slowly turns into a full 3D dragon!

cards.title: Myth: You need 3D training data
cards.body: Myth: To make good 3D objects with AI, you must train on lots of 3D models. Truth: SDS lets you reuse a 2D image diffusion model (trained only on flat pictures) as the judge, and it still guides the creation of a full, consistent 3D object — no 3D dataset required!

cards.title: One Render vs. Many Renders
cards.body: SDS never judges the 3D object directly — it only ever judges flat renders, but from MANY different angles, so the whole 3D shape gets shaped up.

left.text: Looking real from the front only — could hide a messy, broken back or sides

right.text: Looking real from front, back, top, and sides — forces the whole 3D shape to be consistent and correct

exercises.prompt: What does Score Distillation Sampling mainly use to guide the creation of a 3D object?

options.text: A pretrained 2D image diffusion model judging rendered views
A database of finished 3D models to copy from
A physics engine that simulates gravity
A human artist manually sculpting the shape

exercises.explain: SDS uses a 2D diffusion model as a judge for renders from many angles, without needing any 3D training data at all.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Score Distillation Sampling requires a large dataset of 3D models to train on.
exercises.explain: False! SDS cleverly reuses a 2D diffusion model as its guide, so no 3D dataset is needed at all.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the idea behind SDS.
exercises.sentence: SDS renders the 3D object from many ___ and checks each render with a 2D diffusion model.
exercises.explain: By rendering from many different camera angles and checking each one, SDS makes sure the entire 3D shape looks correct, not just one side.

exercises.prompt: Why does SDS render the 3D object from MANY different camera angles instead of just one?

options.text: So the whole 3D shape becomes consistent and realistic from every side, not just one view
Because rendering from one angle is technically impossible
To make the file size of the 3D model smaller

exercises.explain: If only one angle were checked, the rest of the 3D object could look broken or messy. Many angles ensure the object is convincing all around.

exercises.prompt: A student says: 'Score Distillation Sampling is basically training a brand-new 2D image model from scratch and then using it once to draw a 3D dragon in a single flat picture.' What is WRONG with this statement?

options.text: SDS reuses an already-pretrained 2D diffusion model as a judge, and it renders + checks the 3D object from MANY angles repeatedly, not just once in a single flat picture
Nothing is wrong, the statement is completely correct
SDS only works with black and white images
SDS actually trains a new 3D model from scratch every time

exercises.explain: SDS does NOT train a new 2D model from scratch — it reuses an existing pretrained 2D diffusion model. And it doesn't just make one flat picture; it repeatedly renders the 3D object from many angles and uses feedback each time to slowly refine the actual 3D shape.
